{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所観音}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996.
== See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)}} * [https://www.inadanikankou.jp/spot/page/id=2066 Kenpuku-ji - Nagano Ina Valley Tourism Bureau] * [https://www.takatoishiku.com/erea/kenpukuzi.html Kenpuku-ji Stone Statue Pilgrimage Site - Takato Stonemason Research Center]

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所観音}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996.

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所観音}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996.
== See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)}} * [https://www.inadanikankou.jp/spot/page/id=2066 Kenpuku-ji - Nagano Ina Valley Tourism Bureau] * [https://www.takatoishiku.com/erea/kenpukuzi.html Kenpuku-ji Stone Statue Pilgrimage Site - Takato Stonemason Research Center]

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所観音}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996.

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所観音}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996.
== See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)}} * [https://www.inadanikankou.jp/spot/page/id=2066 Kenpuku-ji - Nagano Ina Valley Tourism Bureau] * [https://www.takatoishiku.com/erea/kenpukuzi.html Kenpuku-ji Stone Statue Pilgrimage Site - Takato Stonemason Research Center]

== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).

The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.

'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).

The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. {{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所観音}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996.
== See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)}} * [https://www.inadanikankou.jp/spot/page/id=2066 Kenpuku-ji - Nagano Ina Valley Tourism Bureau] * [https://www.takatoishiku.com/erea/kenpukuzi.html Kenpuku-ji Stone Statue Pilgrimage Site - Takato Stonemason Research Center]

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所観音}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996.
== See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)}} * [https://www.inadanikankou.jp/spot/page/id=2066 Kenpuku-ji - Nagano Ina Valley Tourism Bureau] * [https://www.takatoishiku.com/erea/kenpukuzi.html Kenpuku-ji Stone Statue Pilgrimage Site - Takato Stonemason Research Center]

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所観音}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996.== See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)}} * [https://www.inadanikankou.jp/spot/page/id=2066 Kenpuku-ji - Nagano Ina Valley Tourism Bureau] * [https://www.takatoishiku.com/erea/kenpukuzi.html Kenpuku-ji Stone Statue Pilgrimage Site - Takato Stonemason Research Center]

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所観音}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996. == See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)}} * [https://www.inadanikankou.jp/spot/page/id=2066 Kenpuku-ji - Nagano Ina Valley Tourism Bureau] * [https://www.takatoishiku.com/erea/kenpukuzi.html Kenpuku-ji Stone Statue Pilgrimage Site - Takato Stonemason Research Center]

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所観音}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996. == See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)}} * [https://www.inadanikankou.jp/spot/page/id=2066 Kenpuku-ji - Nagano Ina Valley Tourism Bureau] * [https://www.takatoishiku.com/erea/kenpukuzi.html Kenpuku-ji Stone Statue Pilgrimage Site - Takato Stonemason Research Center]

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|12=draft}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗|12=draft}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|12=draft}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚|12=draft}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場|12=draft}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗|12=draft}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|12=draft}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆|12=draft}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|12=draft}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)|12=draft}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼|12=draft}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城|12=draft}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=draft}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏|12=draft}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)|12=draft}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以|12=draft}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|12=draft}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所観音|12=draft}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏|12=draft}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996. == See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|12=draft}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)}} * [https://www.inadanikankou.jp/spot/page/id=2066 Kenpuku-ji - Nagano Ina Valley Tourism Bureau] * [https://www.takatoishiku.com/erea/kenpukuzi.html Kenpuku-ji Stone Statue Pilgrimage Site - Takato Stonemason Research Center]
{{citation needed|Kenpuku-ji}}

revision.text:
{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|12=draft}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗|12=draft}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|12=draft}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚|12=draft}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場|12=draft}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗|12=draft}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|12=draft}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆|12=draft}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|12=draft}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)|12=draft}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼|12=draft}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城|12=draft}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=draft}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏|12=draft}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)|12=draft}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以|12=draft}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|12=draft}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所観音|12=draft}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏|12=draft}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996.
== See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|12=draft}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)}} * [https://www.inadanikankou.jp/spot/page/id=2066 Kenpuku-ji - Nagano Ina Valley Tourism Bureau] * [https://www.takatoishiku.com/erea/kenpukuzi.html Kenpuku-ji Stone Statue Pilgrimage Site - Takato Stonemason Research Center]
{{citation needed|Kenpuku-ji}}

<!--Kenpuku-ji-->

revision.text:
{{otheruses}} {{日本の寺院 |名称 = 建福寺 |画像 = [[ファイル:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] |所在地 = [[長野県]][[伊那市]]高遠町西高遠1824 | 緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 50|緯度秒 = 17 | 経度度 = 138|経度分 = 03|経度秒 = 24 |山号 = 大宝山 |宗旨 = [[臨済宗]] |宗派 = 妙心寺派 |寺格 = |本尊 = [[釈迦如来]] |創建年 =（伝）平安時代末〜鎌倉時代初 |開基 =（伝）[[文覚]] |正式名 = 大宝山 建福寺 |別称 = |札所等 = [[伊那諏訪八十八霊場]]第51番札所 |文化財 = 紙本墨画中観音左右竜虎図（国の重要文化財） |地図2 = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=建福寺|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''建福寺'''（けんぷくじ）は、[[長野県]][[伊那市]]高遠町西高遠にある[[臨済宗妙心寺派]]の寺院である。
==歴史== 伝承によれば[[文覚]]上人（[[1139年]]～[[1203年]]）が独鈷池付近に開創。 [[康元]]元年（[[1256年]]）[[蘭渓道隆]]（大覚禅師）が立ち寄り、鉾持山乾福興国禅寺（ほこじさんけんぷくこうこくぜんじ）を興した。 のち、[[武田信玄]]の帰依により、[[静岡市]]の[[臨済寺 (静岡市)|臨済寺]]から東谷宗杲禅師を招いて中興開山とし、妙心寺派となった。 [[貞享]]2年（[[1685年]]）、喝道和尚中興、[[明和]]3年（[[1765年]]）3月24日類焼、[[安永]]4年（[[1775年]]）少山和尚がさらに中興するも、[[嘉永]]4年（[[1851年]]）正月19日焼失、[[安政]]3年（[[1856年]]）に再建した。
[[武田勝頼]]が[[高遠城]]主の時、武田家菩提所とし、母[[諏訪御料人]]を埋葬して乾福院殿という。 さらに次の藩主、[[保科氏]]も菩提寺とし、大寶山建福寺と改名。 [[寛永]]13年（[[1636年]]）[[保科正之]]が会津若松藩主となり、移封する際に、鉄舟和尚が供奉して[[会津]]にも[[建福寺 (会津若松市)]]を創建した。
==文化財== {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = 紙本墨画観音竜虎図（国重文）
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | alt1 = | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | alt2 = | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | alt3 = | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|石仏群（市指定）]] === 重要文化財（国指定） === *紙本墨画観音竜虎図 :江戸初期の画家[[狩野興以]]筆の墨絵である。[[重要文化財]]指定名称は「紙本墨画中観音左右竜虎図」。三幅対で構成され、中幅に白衣観音、その左右に龍と虎が配されている。一幅の縦は167.3cm・幅は106.1cmである。大正12年（1923年）3月28日に重要文化財に指定された<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 八十二文化財団]ほか諸資料</ref>。
=== その他 === *建福寺の石仏群 :本堂への階段へ上る石段の両側および境内には、[[江戸時代]]の[[高遠石工]]によって作られた45体の石仏がある。45体の石仏のうち、西国三十三観音が33体、延命地蔵1体、願王地蔵1体、六地蔵6体、不動明王1体、楊柳観音1体、法陀羅山地蔵1体、仏足石などがある。<ref>『高遠町誌 上 歴史 二』 昭和58年3月30日 高遠町誌刊行会</ref> *保科家 :保科氏は二代将軍の子、正之を養子とし会津若松城主に栄転の節には勉学の師である第五世鉄船和尚が共奉し会津に建福寺を創建する。当時は群下に末寺八ヶ寺を有する。<ref>『上伊那の寺院 上伊那仏教会編』 平成九年八月一日 上伊那仏教会 P146</ref>
==脚注== {{Reflist}}
==参考文献== *『上伊那郡史』 昭和48年6月7日 中村安孝 *『高遠町誌 上 歴史 二』 昭和58年3月30日 高遠町誌刊行会 *『高遠町誌 下 自然・現代・民俗』 昭和54年7月10日 高遠町誌刊行会 *『伊那市ふるさと百科 あすの暮らしを育む文化百科』平成19年1月1日 株式会社新葉社 *『上伊那の寺院 上伊那仏教会編』 平成九年八月一日 上伊那仏教会 *『探訪 信州の古寺 禅宗』1996年 [[郷土出版社]]
== 関連項目 == *[[高遠石工]]
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)}} * [https://www.inadanikankou.jp/spot/page/id=2066 建福寺 - 長野伊那谷観光局] *[https://www.takatoishiku.com/erea/kenpukuzi.html 建福寺「高遠石工」石仏巡礼の聖地 - 高遠石工研究センター]
{{DEFAULTSORT:けんふくし}} [[Category:臨済宗妙心寺派の寺院]] [[Category:長野県の寺]] [[Category:伊那市の寺]] [[Category:伊那市の歴史]] [[Category:長野県の重要文化財]] [[Category:伊那市の建築物]]

==文化財== {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = 紙本墨画観音竜虎図（国重文）
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | alt1 = | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | alt2 = | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | alt3 = | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|石仏群（市指定）]] === 重要文化財（国指定） === *紙本墨画観音竜虎図 :江戸初期の画家[[狩野興以]]筆の墨絵である。[[重要文化財]]指定名称は「紙本墨画中観音左右竜虎図」。三幅対で構成され、中幅に白衣観音、その左右に龍と虎が配されている。一幅の縦は167.3cm・幅は106.1cmである。大正12年（1923年）3月28日に重要文化財に指定された<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 八十二文化財団]ほか諸資料</ref>。

revision.text:
{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=draft観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996.
== See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)}} * [https://www.inadanikankou.jp/spot/page/id=2066 Kenpuku-ji - Nagano Ina Valley Tourism Bureau] * [https://www.takatoishiku.com/erea/kenpukuzi.html Kenpuku-ji Stone Statue Pilgrimage Site - Takato Stonemason Research Center]
{{translated page|ja|建福寺|version=101735544|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina City|qq=}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinzai Zen|qq=}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Buddha|qq=}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monk Mongaku|qq=}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|qq=}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinzai Zen|qq=}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina City|qq=}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rankei Dōryū|qq=}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|qq=}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|qq=}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Katsuyori|qq=}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Castle|qq=}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|qq=}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoshina clan|qq=}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|qq=}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanō Koi|qq=}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato stonemasons|qq=}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=draft観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoshina clan|qq=}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996.
== See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato stonemasons|qq=}}

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina City|qq=}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinzai Zen|qq=}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Buddha|qq=}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Monk Mongaku|ja|文覚|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monk Mongaku|qq=}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|qq=}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinzai Zen|qq=}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina City|qq=}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk [[:en:Mongaku|Mongaku]] (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Rankei Dōryū|ja|蘭渓道隆|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rankei Dōryū|qq=}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|qq=}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|ja|臨済寺 (静岡市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|qq=}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Katsuyori|qq=}} as lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Castle|qq=}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|qq=}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoshina clan|qq=}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|qq=}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanō Koi|qq=}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato stonemasons|qq=}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|西国三十三所|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=draft観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|ja|保科氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoshina clan|qq=}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
