{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina City|1=Ina, Nagano}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai Zen|1=Rinzai school}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Mongaku|en|Mongaku|WD=Q11080166|lt=Monk Mongaku|1=Mongaku}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|1=Ina Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q128866411}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai Zen|1=Rinzai school}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina City|1=Ina, Nagano}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk {{ill|Mongaku|en|Mongaku|WD=Q11080166|lt=Mongaku|1=Mongaku}} (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Lanxi Daolong|en|Lanxi Daolong|WD=Q357988|lt=Rankei Dōryū|1=Lanxi Daolong}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji|en|Rinzai-ji|WD=Q11612559|lt=Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|1=Rinzai-ji}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|1=Takeda Katsuyori}} as lord of {{ill|Takatō Castle|en|Takatō Castle|WD=Q11672954|lt=Takato Castle|1=Takatō Castle}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|1=Suwa Goronin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|en|Hoshina clan|WD=Q2661264|lt=Hoshina clan|1=Hoshina clan}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanō Koi|1=Kanō Kōi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571484}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato stonemasons|1=Takato stonemasons|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20041103}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|en|Hoshina clan|WD=Q2661264|lt=Hoshina clan|1=Hoshina clan}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)]][[ja:建福寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)]][[ja:建福寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)]][[ja:建福寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:建福寺2015.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667|1=Myōshin-ji sect}}|prefecture={{ill|Ina|WD=Q840888|1=Ina, Nagano}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.838055555555556|N|138.05666666666667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}
{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina City|1=Ina, Nagano}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai Zen|1=Rinzai school}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Mongaku|en|Mongaku|WD=Q11080166|lt=Monk Mongaku|1=Mongaku}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|1=Ina Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q128866411}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai Zen|1=Rinzai school}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina City|1=Ina, Nagano}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk {{ill|Mongaku|en|Mongaku|WD=Q11080166|lt=Mongaku|1=Mongaku}} (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Lanxi Daolong|en|Lanxi Daolong|WD=Q357988|lt=Rankei Dōryū|1=Lanxi Daolong}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji|en|Rinzai-ji|WD=Q11612559|lt=Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|1=Rinzai-ji}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|1=Takeda Katsuyori}} as lord of {{ill|Takatō Castle|en|Takatō Castle|WD=Q11672954|lt=Takato Castle|1=Takatō Castle}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|1=Suwa Goronin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|en|Hoshina clan|WD=Q2661264|lt=Hoshina clan|1=Hoshina clan}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanō Koi|1=Kanō Kōi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571484}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato stonemasons|1=Takato stonemasons|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20041103}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|en|Hoshina clan|WD=Q2661264|lt=Hoshina clan|1=Hoshina clan}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996.
== See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato stonemasons|1=Takato stonemasons|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20041103}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Kenpuku-ji (Ina, Nagano)}} * [https://www.inadanikankou.jp/spot/page/id=2066 Kenpuku-ji - Nagano Ina Valley Tourism Bureau] * [https://www.takatoishiku.com/erea/kenpukuzi.html Kenpuku-ji Stone Statue Pilgrimage Site - Takato Stonemason Research Center]

=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato stonemasons|1=Takato stonemasons|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q20041103}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qid=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|en|Hoshina clan|qid=Q2661264|lt=Hoshina clan|1=Hoshina clan}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nakamura, Yasukō. ''Upper Ina History,'' 1973. * ''Takatō Town History, Volumes 1 & 2,'' Takatō Town History Publication Committee, 1983. * ''Ina City Local Encyclopedia,'' Shin'yōsha, 2007. * ''Exploring Shinano's Ancient Zen Temples,'' Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1996.
== See Also == * {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato stonemasons|1=Takato stonemasons|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q20041103}}

{{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:建福寺2015.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|qid=Q108821667|1=Myōshin-ji sect}}|prefecture={{ill|Ina|qid=Q840888|1=Ina, Nagano}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.838055555555556|N|138.05666666666667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}
{{otheruses}} {{Infobox Japanese Temple | name = Kenpuku-ji (建福寺) | image = [[File:建福寺2015.JPG|270px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|qid=Q840888|lt=Ina City|1=Ina, Nagano}}, Takato Town Nishi-Takato 1824 | coordinates = {{coord|35|50|17|N|138|03|24|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | mountain_name = Daihōzan (大宝山) | sect = {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|lt=Rinzai Zen|1=Rinzai school}} Myoshin-ji School (妙心寺派) | main_hall = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} | founded = (Traditional) Late Heian to Early Kamakura period | founder = (Traditional) {{ill|Mongaku|en|Mongaku|qid=Q11080166|lt=Monk Mongaku|1=Mongaku}} | cultural_properties = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property) | pilgrimage_sites = {{ill|Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|ja|伊那諏訪八十八霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina-Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|1=Ina Suwa 88 Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q128866411}} No. 51 | map = {{Location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|label=Kenpuku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kenpuku-ji''' (建福寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|lt=Rinzai Zen|1=Rinzai school}} temple of the Myoshin-ji School located in {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|qid=Q840888|lt=Ina City|1=Ina, Nagano}}, Nagano Prefecture.
== History == According to tradition, the temple was founded by the monk {{ill|Mongaku|en|Mongaku|qid=Q11080166|lt=Mongaku|1=Mongaku}} (1139–1203) near Dokko Pond. In 1256 (Kōgen 1), Zen master {{ill|Lanxi Daolong|en|Lanxi Daolong|qid=Q357988|lt=Rankei Dōryū|1=Lanxi Daolong}} (Daikaku Zenji) visited and established the temple under the name Hokojisankempukukokokuzenji (鉾持山乾福興国禅寺).
Later, under the patronage of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Zen master Sōkō Zenji was invited from {{ill|Rinzai-ji|en|Rinzai-ji|qid=Q11612559|lt=Rinzai-ji (Shizuoka City)|1=Rinzai-ji}} to revitalize the temple. The temple became affiliated with the Myoshin-ji School. Kenpuku-ji underwent several reconstructions, including major rebuilding in 1685 (Jōkyō 2) by Katsu Dō Oshō, and again in 1856 (Ansei 3) after a fire.
During the tenure of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|qid=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|1=Takeda Katsuyori}} as lord of {{ill|Takatō Castle|en|Takatō Castle|qid=Q11672954|lt=Takato Castle|1=Takatō Castle}}, the temple served as the Takeda family mausoleum and housed the remains of his mother, {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|1=Suwa Goronin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8193299}}, under the temple name Kenpuku-in-den (乾福院殿). When the {{ill|Hoshina clan|en|Hoshina clan|qid=Q2661264|lt=Hoshina clan|1=Hoshina clan}} became the ruling family, the temple was renamed Daihōzan Kenpuku-ji. In 1636 (Kan'ei 13), when Hoshina Masayuki was transferred to the Aizu Domain, a related temple, {{ill|Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja|建福寺 (会津若松市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji (Aizu)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was founded there by Zen master Tetsushū.
== Cultural Properties == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | footer = Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger (National Important Cultural Property)
| image1 = Tiger by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width1 = 89 | caption1 =
| image2 = Kannon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width2 = 95 | caption2 =
| image3 = Dragon by Kano Koi (Kenpukuji Ina).png | width3 = 101 | caption3 = }} [[File:建福寺 石像.jpg|thumb|180px|Stone statues (Municipal Cultural Property)]] === Important Cultural Property (National) === * Ink painting of Kannon flanked by a dragon and tiger : This ink painting, attributed to the early Edo period artist {{ill|Kanō Koi|ja|狩野興以|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanō Koi|1=Kanō Kōi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11571484}}, features Kannon flanked by a dragon and a tiger in a three-panel composition. Each panel measures 167.3 cm in height and 106.1 cm in width. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property on March 28, 1923.<ref>[http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=2196&seq=0 82 Bunka Zaidan] and other sources.</ref>
=== Other Notable Features === * Stone statues of Kenpuku-ji : The temple grounds and the approach to the main hall feature 45 stone statues created by the renowned {{ill|Takato stonemasons|ja|高遠石工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato stonemasons|1=Takato stonemasons|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q20041103}} during the Edo period. These include 33 statues of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qid=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}, six Jizō statues, and statues of Fudō Myōō and other deities.<ref>''Takatō Town History, Volume 2'' (1983), Takatō Town History Publication Committee.</ref> * Hoshina Family Mausoleum : The {{ill|Hoshina clan|en|Hoshina clan|qid=Q2661264|lt=Hoshina clan|1=Hoshina clan}} used Kenpuku-ji as their family temple. When Hoshina Masayuki became lord of the Aizu Domain, he established another Kenpuku-ji temple in Aizu.<ref>''Buddhist Temples of Upper Ina,'' Upper Ina Buddhist Association, 1997, p. 146.</ref>
